D.name: D
D.definition: The minimum depth of a deterministic decision tree that computes $f:\{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ correctly on all inputs. Equivalently, the minimum number of input bits that must be queried in the worst case by any deterministic adaptive algorithm computing $f$.
D.information: The most fundamental query complexity measure, and an upper bound for all standard measures. Every total Boolean function satisfies $D(f) \leq n$. Known polynomial upper bounds include $D(f) \leq C_0(f) \cdot C_1(f) \leq C(f)^2$ [Mon14] and $D(f) = O(Q(f)^6)$ [ABKRT21].

R0.name: R0
R0.definition: Also called the Las Vegas query complexity. The minimum worst-case expected number of queries made by a randomized decision tree that always outputs the correct answer on all inputs. The algorithm must always be correct, but may use randomness to decide which variables to query.
R0.information: Sometimes written $\mathsf{ZPP}(f)$. Satisfies $R(f) \leq R_0(f) \leq D(f)$. Saks and Wigderson [SW86] proved $R_0(f) \geq \Omega(\sqrt{D(f)})$ for all total Boolean functions, implying $D(f) = O(R_0(f)^2)$. This bound is tight: the AND-OR tree achieves $R_0 = \Theta(\sqrt{D})$.

R1.name: R1
R1.definition:
The minimum worst-case number of queries made by a randomized decision tree with one-sided error computing $f$. Concretely:
  • On inputs $x$ with $f(x) = 1$, the algorithm accepts with probability at least $2/3$.
  • On inputs $x$ with $f(x) = 0$, the algorithm always rejects (no false positives).
R1.information: The one-sided error analogue of $R(f)$. Any one-sided algorithm is also a bounded-error algorithm, so $R(f) \leq R_1(f)$. Similarly, any zero-error algorithm is one-sided, so $R_1(f) \leq R_0(f)$. The function OR satisfies $R_1(\text{OR}_n) = \Theta(n)$ while $R(\text{OR}_n) = \Theta(\sqrt{n})$.

R.name: R
R.definition:
The minimum worst-case number of queries made by a bounded-error randomized decision tree computing $f$. Concretely: the algorithm makes at most $T$ queries and satisfies
  • On inputs $x$ with $f(x) = 1$, the algorithm accepts with probability at least $2/3$.
  • On inputs $x$ with $f(x) = 0$, the algorithm rejects with probability at least $2/3$.
R.information: Also written $R_2(f)$ or $\mathsf{BPP}(f)$. The error probability $1/3$ can be replaced by any constant $\epsilon \in (0,1/2)$ with only a constant overhead via amplification. Satisfies $Q(f) \leq R(f) \leq D(f)$. The polynomial method gives $R(f) \geq \widetilde{\deg}(f)$ [BBC+01].

Q.name: Q
Q.definition: The minimum number of quantum queries to the input oracle needed to compute $f$ with bounded error. The input $x \in \{0,1\}^n$ is accessed via a unitary oracle $O_x$ that maps $|i\rangle|b\rangle \mapsto |i\rangle|b \oplus x_i\rangle$. The algorithm is an adaptive sequence of unitary operations and oracle calls, and must output $f(x)$ with probability at least $2/3$.
Q.information: Also written $Q_2(f)$. Satisfies $\widetilde{\deg}(f) \leq Q(f) \leq R(f)$ [BBC+01]. By Reichardt's [Rei11] tight adversary bound theorem, $Q(f) = \Theta(\mathsf{Adv}^{\pm}(f))$ exactly characterizes quantum query complexity. The polynomial method [BBC+01] and the adversary method [Amb02] are the two main lower bound techniques.

QE.name: QE
QE.definition: The minimum number of quantum queries to the input oracle needed to compute $f$ exactly, with no error, on all inputs.
QE.information: Also written $Q_0(f)$ or $\mathsf{EQP}(f)$. Satisfies $Q(f) \leq Q_E(f) \leq D(f)$. By the polynomial method of Beals et al. [BBC+01], $Q_E(f) \geq \lceil \deg(f)/2 \rceil$, so $\deg(f) \leq 2 Q_E(f)$. Sometimes exponentially more powerful than deterministic: the parity function satisfies $Q_E(\text{PAR}_n) = \lceil n/2 \rceil$ but $D(\text{PAR}_n) = n$.

N.name: N
N.definition: The minimum cost of a nondeterministic decision tree computing $f$, where cost is the maximum depth of an accepting path. Equivalently, $N(f) = C_1(f)$: the worst-case minimum number of bits that certify a $1$-input of $f$. Concretely, $N(f) = \max_{x: f(x)=1} \min_{S \subseteq [n]} \{|S| : f(y)=1 \text{ for all } y \text{ with } y_S = x_S\}$.
N.information: Satisfies $N(f) = C_1(f)$ (they are definitionally equivalent). Also $N(f) \leq R_1(f)$ (any one-sided algorithm certifies YES instances). The co-nondeterministic complexity $\mathsf{coN}(f) = C_0(f)$ analogously certifies NO instances. An important bound: $D(f) \leq N(f) \cdot \mathsf{coN}(f)$ [Nis91].

coN.name: coN
coN.definition: The minimum cost of a co-nondeterministic decision tree computing $f$, where cost is the maximum depth of an accepting path. Equivalently, $\mathsf{coN}(f) = C_0(f)$: the worst-case minimum number of bits that certify a $0$-input of $f$.
coN.information: Satisfies $\mathsf{coN}(f) = C_0(f)$. Note $\mathsf{coN}(f) = N(\neg f)$ where $\neg f$ is the complement function. An important bound: $D(f) \leq N(f) \cdot \mathsf{coN}(f)$ [Nis91].

C.name: C
C.definition: A certificate for input $x$ is a set $S \subseteq [n]$ of indices such that all $y$ agreeing with $x$ on $S$ satisfy $f(y) = f(x)$. The certificate complexity of $x$ is $C(f,x) = \min\{|S| : S \text{ is a certificate for } x\}$. The certificate complexity of $f$ is $C(f) = \max_x C(f,x)$.
C.information: Satisfies $C(f) = \max(C_0(f), C_1(f)) = \max(\mathsf{coN}(f), N(f))$. Also $\mathsf{bs}(f) \leq C(f) \leq D(f)$ and $D(f) \leq C(f)^2$ [Mon14]. Known to be polynomially equivalent to all other standard combinatorial measures ($\mathsf{bs}$, $s$, $D$, $R$) [ABKRT21, Hua19].

C1.name: C1
C1.definition: The worst-case certificate complexity over all $1$-inputs: $C_1(f) = \max_{x: f(x) = 1} C(f, x)$. Equivalently, the minimum number of queries needed in the worst case to verify a YES instance nondeterministically.
C1.information: Equals $N(f)$ (the nondeterministic query complexity) by definition. Satisfies $C_1(f) \leq C(f)$ and $C_1(f) \leq D(f)$. The complement satisfies $C_1(\neg f) = C_0(f)$.

C0.name: C0
C0.definition: The worst-case certificate complexity over all $0$-inputs: $C_0(f) = \max_{x: f(x) = 0} C(f, x)$. Equivalently, the minimum number of queries needed in the worst case to verify a NO instance nondeterministically.
C0.information: Equals $\mathsf{coN}(f)$ by definition. Satisfies $C_0(f) \leq C(f)$ and $C_0(f) \leq D(f)$. The complement satisfies $C_0(\neg f) = C_1(f)$.

bs.name: bs
bs.definition: A block $B \subseteq [n]$ is sensitive for $f$ at input $x$ if $f(x^B) \neq f(x)$, where $x^B$ flips all bits of $x$ in $B$. The block sensitivity of $f$ at $x$ is $\mathsf{bs}(f,x) = $ the maximum number of disjoint sensitive blocks at $x$. The block sensitivity of $f$ is $\mathsf{bs}(f) = \max_x \mathsf{bs}(f,x)$.
bs.information: Introduced by Nisan [Nis91]. Satisfies $s(f) \leq \mathsf{bs}(f) \leq C(f) \leq D(f)$. The long-standing Sensitivity Conjecture, proved by Huang [Hua19], showed $\mathsf{bs}(f) \leq s(f)^2$, establishing that all standard combinatorial measures are polynomially equivalent. Nisan and Szegedy [NS94] showed $\deg(f) \geq \sqrt{\mathsf{bs}(f)}$, so $\mathsf{bs}(f) \leq \deg(f)^2$.

fbs.name: fbs
fbs.definition: The fractional relaxation of block sensitivity. Formally, $\mathsf{fbs}(f) = \max_x \mathsf{fbs}(f,x)$, where $\mathsf{fbs}(f,x)$ is the optimal value of a fractional block sensitivity LP: maximize $\sum_B w_B$ over weights $w_B \geq 0$ on sensitive blocks $B$ at $x$, subject to $\sum_{B \ni i} w_B \leq 1$ for all $i \in [n]$.
fbs.information: Defined by Tal [Tal13]. Satisfies $\mathsf{bs}(f) \leq \mathsf{fbs}(f) \leq C(f)$ for all total Boolean functions. Also known to satisfy $\mathsf{fbs}(f) = \mathsf{RC}(f)$ (the randomized certificate complexity), connecting it to randomized complexity. Additionally, $\widetilde{\deg}(f)^2 = \Theta(\mathsf{fbs}(f))$ [Tal13].

s.name: s
s.definition: The sensitivity of $f$ at input $x$ is $s(f,x) = |\{i \in [n] : f(x) \neq f(x^{(i)})\}|$, the number of input bits whose individual flip changes $f(x)$. The sensitivity of $f$ is $s(f) = \max_x s(f,x)$.
s.information: The weakest standard combinatorial measure: $s(f) \leq \mathsf{bs}(f) \leq C(f) \leq D(f)$. For decades it was open whether $s$ and $\mathsf{bs}$ are polynomially related (the Sensitivity Conjecture). This was resolved by Huang [Hua19], who proved $\mathsf{bs}(f) \leq s(f)^2$, implying all of $s, \mathsf{bs}, C, D, R, Q$ are polynomially equivalent.

deg.name: deg
deg.definition: Every Boolean function $f: \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ has a unique multilinear representation as a real polynomial $p: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $p(x) = f(x)$ for all $x \in \{0,1\}^n$. The polynomial degree of $f$ is $\deg(f) = \deg(p)$.
deg.information: Satisfies $\deg(f) \leq D(f)$ (a depth-$D$ decision tree gives a polynomial of degree $\leq D$). Nisan and Szegedy [NS94] proved $\deg(f) \geq \sqrt{\mathsf{bs}(f)}$, so $\mathsf{bs}(f) \leq \deg(f)^2$. Also $\widetilde{\deg}(f) \leq \deg(f)$ trivially. The polynomial degree and approximate degree are both algebraic lower bounds for quantum query complexity: $\lceil \deg(f)/2 \rceil \leq Q_E(f)$ [BBC+01].

adeg.name: adeg
adeg.definition: The approximate degree of $f$ is $\widetilde{\deg}(f) = \min\{\deg(p) : p$ is a real polynomial with $|p(x) - f(x)| \leq 1/3$ for all $x \in \{0,1\}^n\}$.
adeg.information: Defined by Nisan and Szegedy [NS94]. A foundational lower bound for quantum query complexity: $\widetilde{\deg}(f) \leq Q(f)$ for all $f$ [BBC+01]. Known to satisfy $\widetilde{\deg}(f) \geq \sqrt{\mathsf{bs}(f)}$ [She13], so $\mathsf{bs}(f) \leq \widetilde{\deg}(f)^2$. Also $\widetilde{\deg}(f)^2 = \Theta(\mathsf{fbs}(f))$ [Tal13]. The OR function has $\widetilde{\deg}(\text{OR}_n) = \Theta(\sqrt{n})$.

Adv.name: Adv
Adv.definition: Given an adversary matrix $\Gamma$ (a real symmetric matrix indexed by inputs, with $\Gamma[x,y] = 0$ whenever $f(x) = f(y)$), the adversary bound is $\mathsf{Adv}(\Gamma) = \|\Gamma\| / \max_i \|\Gamma \circ D_i\|$, where $D_i[x,y] = |x_i - y_i|$ and $\circ$ is the Hadamard product. The general adversary bound is $\mathsf{Adv}^{\pm}(f) = \max_{\Gamma} \mathsf{Adv}(\Gamma)$ over all real symmetric $\Gamma$ with $\Gamma[x,y]=0$ when $f(x)=f(y)$.
Adv.information: Introduced by Høyer, Lee, and Špalek [HLS07] as a generalization allowing negative weights. The fundamental theorem of Reichardt [Rei11] proves $Q(f) = \Theta(\mathsf{Adv}^{\pm}(f))$ for all total Boolean functions, characterizing bounded-error quantum query complexity exactly via the adversary bound. This implies $Q$ and $\mathsf{Adv}^{\pm}$ are equal up to constant factors.

UPP.name: UPP
UPP.definition: The minimum number of queries made by a randomized decision tree that outputs the correct answer with probability strictly greater than $1/2$ on all inputs. No bound on the gap above $1/2$ is required.
UPP.information: Also called the PP query complexity. Satisfies $\mathsf{UPP}(f) \leq Q(f) \leq R(f) \leq D(f)$. Characterised algebraically: $\mathsf{UPP}(f) = \lceil \deg_{\pm}(f) / 2 \rceil$ where $\deg_{\pm}(f)$ is the minimum degree of a real polynomial $p$ with $\text{sign}(p(x)) = (-1)^{f(x)}$ for all $x \in \{0,1\}^n$ [Bei93]. Can be exponentially smaller than $Q(f)$: the Forrelation function separates $\mathsf{UPP}$ and $Q$ [AA15].

coR1.name: coR1
coR1.definition:
The minimum worst-case number of queries made by a randomized decision tree with co-one-sided error:
  • On inputs $x$ with $f(x) = 0$, the algorithm rejects with probability at least $2/3$.
  • On inputs $x$ with $f(x) = 1$, the algorithm always accepts (no false negatives).
coR1.information: Satisfies $R(f) \leq \mathsf{coR_1}(f) \leq R_0(f)$. The complement of $R_1$: $\mathsf{coR_1}(f) = R_1(\neg f)$. For the OR function, $\mathsf{coR_1}(\text{OR}_n) = \Theta(1)$ (just query any bit; accept if it's 1) while $R_1(\text{OR}_n) = \Theta(n)$.

degF2.name: degF2
degF2.description: GF(2) Polynomial Degree / Parity Decision Tree Complexity
degF2.definition: Every Boolean function $f:\{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ has a unique multilinear representation as a polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_2$. The GF(2) degree $\deg_{\mathbb{F}_2}(f)$ is the degree of this polynomial. Equivalently, it is the minimum number of queries to XOR (parity) functions of input bits needed by a parity decision tree that computes $f$.
degF2.information: Satisfies $\deg_{\mathbb{F}_2}(f) \leq \deg(f)$ (a real representation can be reduced mod 2). Also $\deg_{\mathbb{F}_2}(f) \leq D(f)$ and $\deg_{\mathbb{F}_2}(f) \geq \log_2(s(f) + 1)$ (related to sensitivity). Parity functions have $\deg_{\mathbb{F}_2}(\text{PAR}_n) = 1$ but $\deg(\text{PAR}_n) = n$, showing the two degree notions can differ maximally.
